steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank sits between two complete sentences: The sand at Balos Beach in Greece is a more unusual [shade, though / shade; though /.] and deposits of crushed coral and other organic matter lend the sand a unique pink hue. Both halves have their own subject and main verb, making them two independent clauses. The filler word though acts as an adverb meaning "however" modifying the first clause, not a subordinating conjunction joining the two.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — shade, though;: Places though as a trailing adverb in the first clause ("shade, however"), then uses a semicolon to correctly separate the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice B — shade, though: Uses only a comma between the two independent clauses, creating a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — shade, though,: The second comma makes though look like a parenthetical, but the sentence still ends with just a comma before the second independent clause — another comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — shade; though: Places the semicolon before though, which turns though into a subordinating conjunction starting the second clause. That makes the second clause a dependent fragment: though deposits of crushed coral. lend the sand a unique pink hue cannot stand alone after a semicolon. ✗
